title: Security Engineering — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Comprehensive deep-dive into Security Engineering at Staff+ FAANG engineering level. Covers security foundations (CIA, STRIDE, zero trust), IAM (RBAC, ABAC, Zanzibar), authentication (OAuth 2.0, OIDC, WebAuthn), secrets management (Vault, envelope encryption, rotation), network security (mTLS, BeyondCorp), application security (OWASP Top 10, XSS/SQLI/CSRF), supply chain security (SBOM, Sigstore), cryptography (algorithms, key management, TLS 1.3), data security (encryption, tokenization, masking), compliance (SOC2, PCI-DSS, FedRAMP, HIPAA), cloud security (AWS/GCP/Azure), incident response (detection, forensics, IR playbooks), container & K8s security, developer security (SAST, DAST, security champions), security culture (bug bounties, red teams), and Staff+ security interview strategy.
details:
Staff+ FAANG Interview Strategy for Security: Security engineering interviews at Staff+ level test (1) Threat modeling — can you systematically find vulnerabilities in any system?, (2) Architectural thinking — can you design secure systems that are also usable and performant?, (3) Incident response judgment — how do you lead under pressure during a security incident?, (4) Depth of knowledge — do you understand cryptography, IAM, and network security at the protocol level?, (5) Cultural influence — can you build a security culture across engineering teams?

Company emphasis: Google: BeyondCorp, cryptographic protocol design, Project Zero. Amazon: Leadership principles in security, IAM depth, responsiveness. Meta: Account security at planetary scale, ML for threat detection. Netflix: Cloud-native security, automation, chaos engineering for security.

Key principle: Security at Staff+ level is about tradeoffs. A perfectly secure system is unusable. A perfectly usable system is insecure. Your job is to find the right balance for each situation — and to build systems where the secure choice is also the easy choice.

sections.title: 1. Security Foundations: Threat Modeling, CIA & Zero Trust
sections.description:
FOUNDATIONS · THREAT MODELING · CIA · ZERO TRUST

Staff+ expectation: Security is not a feature — it’s a property of the entire system. At Staff+ level, you don’t just apply security patterns — you threat-model every architectural decision and design systems that are secure by default.

CIA Triad: Confidentiality — data is accessible only to authorized parties (encryption, access control). Integrity — data is not modified by unauthorized parties (signatures, checksums, immutable logs). Availability — system is accessible when needed (redundancy, DDoS protection, incident response). Staff+ insight: tradeoffs between CIA are the core of security architecture. Strong encryption hurts performance. Access control hurts usability. Your job is to balance them.

STRIDE Threat Model (Microsoft): Spoofing (fake identity), Tampering (modify data), Repudiation (deny action), Information Disclosure (leak data), Denial of Service (crash/exhaust), Elevation of Privilege (unauthorized access). Apply STRIDE to each component: "What’s the worst that can happen if this component is compromised?"

Zero Trust (Google BeyondCorp / BeyondProd): Never trust, always verify. No implicit trust based on network location. Every request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted. Key principles: (1) All traffic is untrusted regardless of source. (2) Access decisions are based on device + user + context. (3) Least privilege: grant minimum required access. (4) Continuous verification: re-evaluate on each request, not just at login.

STRIDE
Microsoft threat modeling framework
BeyondCorp
Google’s zero trust architecture (2014)
Least privilege
Most violated security principle at FAANG

sections.title: 2. Identity & Access Management: AuthN, AuthZ, RBAC & ABAC
sections.description:
IAM · AUTHENTICATION · AUTHORIZATION · RBAC

Staff+ expectation: IAM is the foundation of security at FAANG scale. You must design identity systems that serve billions of users (external IAM) and thousands of services (internal IAM). At Staff+, you own the identity architecture for your org.

Authentication (AuthN) vs Authorization (AuthZ): AuthN = who you are (login, MFA, SSO). AuthZ = what you can do (roles, permissions, policies). Never conflate them. At FAANG, AuthN and AuthZ are separate services (Google: OAuth2 for AuthN, Zanzibar for AuthZ).

RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): Users -> Roles -> Permissions. Simple and widely used. Problem: role explosion at scale (at Amazon, there are 10K+ roles). Mitigation: use attributes (ABAC) for fine-grained control.

ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control): Access decisions based on user attributes (department, clearance), resource attributes (classification, project), and environment attributes (time, location). More flexible but harder to audit. Google’s Zanzibar (2020) is the canonical ABAC system at FAANG scale: "if Alice is a member of group G and group G has role R on resource X, allow."

ModelScaleGranularityAuditFAANG use
DimensionRBACABACZanzibar-style
Roles -> PermissionsAttributes -> PolicyRelationship tuples
Good (1K roles)Hard (attribute explosion)Excellent (10B+ tuples)
CoarseFineFine
EasyHardMedium
LegacyGrowingGoogle, Meta, Airbnb

Staff+ insight: Zanzibar (Google, 2019) is the most influential IAM paper of the decade. It models authorization as relationship tuples: (object, relation, user). Example: (doc:123, viewer, user:alice). Tuples are stored in a consistent key-value store. Check: does user:alice have viewer access to doc:123? Answer: walk the relationship graph. At Google, Zanzibar handles 95% of all authorization checks.

sections.title: 3. Authentication: OAuth 2.0, OIDC, SAML & Passwordless
sections.description:
AUTHENTICATION · OAUTH · OIDC · SSO · MFA

Staff+ expectation: Authentication is the most attacked surface. Design for credential theft, session hijacking, and account takeover. At FAANG, authentication systems handle billions of logins/day and must balance security with user experience.

OAuth 2.0: Authorization framework, not authentication. Four grant types: Authorization Code (most secure, recommended), Client Credentials (machine-to-machine), Device Code (CLI/smart TVs), Refresh Token (long-lived, rotated). Always use PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for public clients (SPAs, mobile apps) to prevent authorization code interception.

OpenID Connect (OIDC): Authentication layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Returns ID Token (JWT) containing user identity claims (sub, email, name). Verify: signature (RS256, ES256), issuer (iss), audience (aud), expiry (exp). At FAANG, OIDC is the standard for SSO.

Password + MFAOAuth2 + OIDC SSOPasswordless (WebAuthn)Magic linkDevice Code
FlowSecurityUXUse case
MediumPoorLegacy enterprise
HighGoodConsumer web apps
Very highExcellentFAANG internal apps
MediumExcellentLow-security apps
HighGoodCLI / smart TVs / IoT

Passwordless (WebAuthn / FIDO2): The FAANG standard for internal auth. Uses public-key cryptography: device generates key pair, sends public key to server. Login: server challenges client to sign with private key (biometric or PIN unlocks key). Phishing-resistant. At Google, all employees use WebAuthn (Titan Security Keys) for internal auth since 2017. Passwords are on the way out. Apple, Google, and Microsoft committed to passkeys (WebAuthn) as the future of auth in 2022.

sections.title: 4. Secrets Management: Encryption, Rotation & Vault
sections.description:
SECRETS · VAULT · ENCRYPTION · ROTATION

Staff+ expectation: Secrets management is one of the highest-risk areas. A leaked secret can give an attacker full access. At FAANG, secrets are never in code, never in config files, and are rotated automatically. You design the secret infrastructure for your org.

Secrets Classification: API keys, database passwords, TLS private keys, encryption keys, service account credentials, cloud provider keys. Each category has different rotation requirements: DB passwords (every 90 days), TLS certs (every 90 days), API keys (on compromise), encryption keys (every 1-2 years for data keys, never for master keys).

HashiCorp Vault:  The industry standard for secrets management. Dynamic secrets: Vault generates credentials on demand (e.g., a database user/password with a TTL of 1 hour). When the lease expires, the credential is revoked. This eliminates long-lived secrets. At FAANG, every service gets dynamic secrets with automatic rotation.

Env variablesConfig files (git)Vault staticVault dynamicCloud KMS (AWS/GCP)
ApproachSecurityOps costRotationFAANG use
LowNoneManualLegacy
Very lowLowManualAnti-pattern
HighMediumAuto (agent)Meta
HighestMediumAuto (lease)Google, Amazon
HighLowAuto (rotation)Cloud-native

Encryption Key Hierarchy (Envelope Encryption): Master Key (KEK) encrypts Data Encryption Keys (DEKs). DEKs encrypt actual data. Master Key is stored in HSM or cloud KMS (never in application memory). DEKs are stored alongside data (encrypted). At AWS KMS: CMK (Customer Master Key) encrypts data keys. At Google Cloud: Cloud KMS key ring encrypts key versions. This hierarchy allows key rotation without re-encrypting all data.

sections.title: 5. Network Security: mTLS, Zero Trust Networks & Firewalls
sections.description:
NETWORK · mTLS · ZERO TRUST · BEYONDCORP

Staff+ expectation: The network perimeter is dead. Modern security assumes every request is hostile, even between services in the same data center. At FAANG, all inter-service communication uses mTLS with automatic certificate rotation.

mTLS (Mutual TLS): Both client and server present TLS certificates. Certificates are short-lived (24 hours) and automatically rotated. SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone) standardizes service identity: spiffe://cluster.local/ns/default/sa/web. SPIRE is the reference implementation: it issues SVIDs (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents) as X.509 certs or JWTs. CPU overhead: 5-15% for TLS termination, mitigated by hardware acceleration (e.g., Intel QAT, AWS Nitro).

BeyondCorp (Google’s Zero Trust Network): No VPN. Access based on device + user + context, not network location. All access goes through an access proxy that verifies: device is managed (corporate device with up-to-date OS), user is authenticated (OIDC with WebAuthn), context is normal (geo, time, behavior). At Google, BeyondCorp has been in use since 2014 and eliminated the need for a corporate VPN.

PerimeterZero Trust (BeyondCorp)Service mesh (Istio)Micro-segmentation
ModelTrust basisNetwork scopeFAANG example
Inside = trustedCorporate network + VPNLegacy enterprise
Device + user + contextInternet (no VPN)Google (since 2014)
mTLS + SPIFFECluster-wideAll FAANGs
Per-workload firewallPods / containersAWS Security Groups

sections.title: 6. Application Security: OWASP Top 10 & Code-Level Defenses
sections.description:
APP SECURITY · OWASP · XSS · SQLI · CSRF

Staff+ expectation: Application security is everyone’s responsibility, but at Staff+ level you design the frameworks and libraries that make it easy for every engineer to write secure code. You build security into the development lifecycle, not bolt it on after deployment.

OWASP Top 10 (2021) — Critical at FAANG:

  • A01: Broken Access Control — most common at FAANG. Example: user A can access user B’s data by changing an ID in the URL. Mitigation: server-side authorization check on every request (never trust client-side).
  • A02: Cryptographic Failures — using weak algorithms (MD5, SHA-1), not encrypting sensitive data. FAANG standard: AES-256-GCM for data, SHA-256 for hashing, TLS 1.3 for transport.
  • A03: Injection — SQL injection at FAANG is rare because of ORM + parameterized queries, but NoSQL injection (MongoDB) and command injection are still problems. Mitigation: never concatenate user input into queries. Use parameterized queries or ORM.
  • A07: Identification and Authentication Failures — weak passwords, no MFA, session fixation. FAANG standard: MFA for all users, WebAuthn for high-value, automatic session invalidation on password change.
SQL InjectionXSSCSRFSSRFIDOR
VulnerabilityFAANG mitigationDetection methodSeverity
Parameterized queries (all code)SAST + DASTCritical
Content-Security-Policy + auto-escaping templatesSAST + scanningHigh
SameSite cookies + CSRF tokensManual reviewMedium
Allow-list egress traffic + URL validationSAST + runtimeHigh
Server-side authorization on every endpointManual pentestCritical

Content Security Policy (CSP): HTTP header that prevents XSS by controlling which resources can be loaded. Example: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://cdn.example.com. At Meta, CSP violations are monitored and alerted. At Google, CSP is enforced on all services.

Staff+ insight: The most effective app security investment at FAANG is security frameworks that make the right thing the easy thing. Auto-escaping templates (React JSX, Django templates), parameterized query builders, CSRF protection built into the web framework. If engineers have to think about security for every endpoint, security will fail.

sections.title: 7. Supply Chain Security: SBOM, Sigstore & Dependency Management
sections.description:
SUPPLY CHAIN · SBOM · SIGSTORE · DEPENDENCIES

Staff+ expectation: Supply chain attacks are the fastest-growing threat vector (SolarWinds 2020, log4shell 2021, xz/utils 2024). At FAANG, every dependency is scanned, signed, and verified. At Staff+ level, you design the supply chain security strategy for your org.

SBOM (Software Bill of Materials): A machine-readable inventory of all components in a software artifact (SPDX or CycloneDX format). Includes: component name, version, license, dependencies, hashes. At Google, every container image has an SBOM generated at build time and stored alongside the image. At Amazon, SBOMs are required for all production services.

Sigstore:  A non-profit standard for signing and verifying software artifacts. Key components: Fulcio (certificate authority for short-lived signing certs), Rekor (transparency log for signatures), Cosign (container signing CLI). At FAANG, all internal packages are signed with Sigstore-style ephemeral keys (the signing key lives for seconds, tied to the CI pipeline identity).

Developer workstationSource controlBuild pipelineDependency resolutionArtifact storageDeployment
Supply chain stageThreatMitigationFAANG tool
Credential theftHardware keys, WebAuthnTitan keys
Malicious commitSigned commits, 2-person reviewGerrit / Phabricator
Compromised buildHermetic builds, provenanceBazel (remote exec)
Malicious packageDependency pinning, scanningRenovate / Dependabot
Tampered artifactSignatures, transparency logSigstore / in-toto
Unauthorized deployDeploy approval, canarySpinnaker / Kayenta

Dependency Management at Scale: FAANG companies manage 10K+ dependencies across monorepos. Strategies: (1) Single version policy: Every dependency is at the same version across the entire monorepo. Avoids diamond dependency conflicts. (2) Automated upgrades: Bots (Renovate, Dependabot) create PRs for dependency updates. Critical security patches are auto-merged after CI passes. (3) Dependency scanning: Every commit scans known vulnerabilities (CVE database). If a dependency has a CVE, the build is blocked.

sections.title: 8. Cryptography: Algorithms, Key Management & TLS Deep Dive
sections.description:
CRYPTO · ALGORITHMS · KEY MANAGEMENT · TLS

Staff+ expectation: Cryptography is the foundation of all security. At Staff+ level, you don’t implement crypto primitives — you choose the right algorithms, manage keys securely, and design protocols that use crypto correctly. The most common crypto mistakes at FAANG are not in the algorithms but in their usage.

AES-256-GCMChaCha20-Poly1305RSA-4096ECDSA P-256Ed25519SHA-256Argon2id
AlgorithmUse caseKey sizeStatus
Data at rest, symmetric encryption256-bitRecommended
Mobile/low-power devices256-bitRecommended
Legacy, key exchange4096-bitDeprecated (use ECDH)
TLS certificates, signatures256-bitRecommended
Fast signatures, modern256-bitRecommended
Hashing, integrity256-bit outputRecommended
Password hashingVariableRecommended

TLS 1.3 Deep Dive: FAANG standard for transport security. TLS 1.3 improvements over 1.2: (1) 1-RTT handshake (vs 2-RTT in 1.2), (2) 0-RTT resumption (for previously connected clients, tradeoff: replay vulnerability), (3) Removed weak ciphers (RC4, 3DES, CBC mode), (4) Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) is mandatory. At Google, TLS 1.3 adoption saved millions of dollars in latency costs and eliminated a class of downgrade attacks.

Key Rotation: Keys must be rotated before they are compromised. Rotation schedules: TLS certs (90 days or less), encryption keys (1 year), signing keys (1 year). At Let’s Encrypt: 90-day cert lifetimes are the standard. At FAANG, internal CA issues certs with 24-hour lifetimes and auto-renews via sidecar process (e.g., cert-manager, SPIRE agent).

sections.title: 9. Data Security: Encryption, Tokenization & Masking
sections.description:
DATA SECURITY · ENCRYPTION · TOKENIZATION · PII

Staff+ expectation: Data is the most valuable asset and the biggest liability. At Staff+, you design data security architectures that protect data at rest, in transit, and in use. You understand data classification, encryption strategies, and the tradeoffs between security and performance.

Data Classification: (1) Public — no protection needed. (2) Internal — access requires authentication. (3) Confidential — encrypted at rest and in transit, access controlled. (4) Restricted — encrypted, audited, need-to-know access, DLP monitoring. At FAANG, data classification is automated: ML models scan data stores and classify based on content.

Encryption at restEncryption in transitTokenizationData maskingFormat-preserving enc
TechniqueDescriptionUse casePerformance impact
AES-256-GCM, key per tenantDatabases, S3, disks~10% overhead
TLS 1.3, mTLSAll inter-service comms5-15% CPU
Replace PII with tokensPCI, GDPR complianceLow
Show partial data (e.g., 1234-****-****-5678)UI, logs, supportMinimal
Encrypt but keep format (e.g., SSN)Legacy systemsMedium

Tokenization: Replace sensitive data (credit card numbers, SSNs) with a non-sensitive token. The mapping is stored in a secure token vault. PCI DSS requires tokenization for credit card storage. At Stripe: card numbers are tokenized immediately. At Amazon: PII is tokenized in all non-production environments.

Data Loss Prevention (DLP): Monitor data access and movement for policy violations. At Google: DLP scans Gmail, Drive, and Cloud Storage for sensitive data patterns (credit card numbers, passports). At Amazon: Macie scans S3 for PII. Staff+ insight: DLP generates massive amounts of alerts. Focus on: (1) data leaving the org (egress), (2) data accessed by unauthorized principals, (3) data stored in unencrypted form.

sections.title: 10. Compliance & Auditing: SOC2, PCI-DSS, FedRAMP & HIPAA
sections.description:
COMPLIANCE · AUDIT · SOC2 · PCI · HIPAA

Staff+ expectation: Compliance is not just a legal requirement — it’s a framework for building secure systems. At FAANG, compliance is embedded in the engineering process: every infrastructure change is auditable, every access is logged, every control is monitored.

SOC 2PCI-DSSFedRAMPHIPAAISO 27001
StandardScopeKey requirementsFAANG relevance
Service orgsSecurity, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacyAll cloud providers
Payment processingEncrypt card data, access control, network segmentationAmazon, Stripe, PayPal
US gov cloudNIST 800-53 controls, third-party assessmentAWS GovCloud, GCP
HealthcarePHI protection, BAAs, audit controlsHealthcare on AWS/GCP
General ISMSRisk assessment, security policy, access controlAll major orgs

Audit Logging: Every security-relevant action must be logged: authentication (success + failure), authorization (grant + deny), data access (read + write), configuration changes, privilege escalation. Logs must be: (1) Tamper-proof (write-once storage, e.g., immutable S3 bucket with Object Lock). (2) Readily available (within 1 hour for incident response). (3) Retained per policy (1 year for general, 7 years for compliance). At AWS CloudTrail: all API calls are logged with 90-day default retention.

FedRAMP / GovCloud:  US government requires FedRAMP authorization for cloud services. Controls include: FIPS 140-2 validated cryptography, data residency within US, background checks for personnel, 24/7 security monitoring. At FAANG, GovCloud regions are physically and logically separated from commercial regions.

sections.title: 11. Cloud Security: AWS, GCP & Azure Security Architecture
sections.description:
CLOUD SECURITY · IAM · GUARD DUTY · WAF

Staff+ expectation: Cloud security is shared responsibility. The cloud provider secures the infrastructure (physical, network, hypervisor). You secure everything above: IAM, network ACLs, data encryption, application security. At FAANG scale, cloud security is managed through Infrastructure as Code with automated policy enforcement.

IAMNetworkData encryptionMonitoringComplianceWAF
LayerAWSGCPAzure
IAM roles/policies + OrganizationsIAM + org policies + CIRTRBAC + Azure AD + Policy
VPCs, Security Groups, NACLsVPC, firewall rules, Cloud ArmorVNet, NSG, Azure Firewall
KMS, S3 SSE, CloudHSMCloud KMS, CMEK, HSMKey Vault, SSE, HSM
CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Security HubCloud Audit Logs, Security Command CenterDefender, Sentinel, Monitor
Artifact, Config, Audit ManagerAssured Workloads, Compliance ReportsPolicy, Blueprints, Compliance
AWS WAF + Shield AdvancedCloud ArmorAzure WAF + DDoS Protection

Cloud Security Principals: (1) Least privilege — every IAM role should have the minimum permissions needed. Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to find over-privileged roles. (2) Defense in depth — network ACLs + security groups + OS firewall + application auth. (3) Immutable infrastructure — no manual changes. Every change goes through IaC (Terraform, Pulumi). (4) Automated policy enforcement — prevent non-compliant resources from being created (e.g., S3 bucket without encryption is denied by SCP).

Service Control Policies (AWS Organizations):  Centrally control the maximum permissions for all accounts in an organization. Example SCP: deny all S3 buckets without encryption. SCPs are the most effective tool for cloud security at scale. At Amazon, SCPs enforce (1) no public S3 buckets, (2) no unencrypted EBS volumes, (3) no root user activity, (4) mandatory CloudTrail in all regions.

sections.title: 12. Security Incident Response: Detection, Forensics & Remediation
sections.description:
INCIDENT RESPONSE · DETECTION · FORENSICS · REMEDIATION

Staff+ expectation: Security incidents are inevitable. At Staff+ level, you design the detection and response infrastructure, lead security incident response, and drive post-incident improvements. Time is the critical resource: faster detection and containment reduce damage exponentially.

Security Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61): Preparation -> Detection & Analysis -> Containment, Eradication & Recovery -> Post-Incident. At FAANG, each phase has specific targets: Detection (MTTD < 1 hour for critical), Containment (MTTC < 15 minutes), Eradication (< 24 hours).

Detection Engineering: Build detections as code. Rules are tested in CI, deployed to SIEM (Splunk, Elastic, Chronicle), tuned based on false positive rate. Detection types: (1) Signature-based — known IOCs (IPs, domains, hashes). (2) Behavioral — anomalous patterns (unusual API calls, data egress spike). (3) ML-based — user and entity behavior analytics (UEBA). At Google, Chronicle processes 100TB+ of security data daily and runs 10K+ detection rules.

DetectionContainmentEradicationRecoveryPost-incident
PhaseKey actionsTargetFAANG tool
Log analysis, alert triageMTTD < 60 minChronicle, Splunk, ES
Isolate host, revoke creds, block IPMTTC < 15 minAutomated playbooks
Remove malware, patch vuln, rotate keys< 24 hoursPatch mgmt + IAM rotation
Restore from backup, verify fixDepends on RTOBackup verification
Root cause, systemic fix, postmortem< 1 weekAction tracker

Forensics: Collect evidence without destroying it. Order of volatility: CPU/memory -> process table -> network connections -> temporary files -> disk. At FAANG: forensic images are captured to immutable storage. Live memory analysis uses LiME (Linux Memory Extractor) or AVML. Disk forensics uses Sleuth Kit / Autopsy. Chain of custody is documented with hash verification.

sections.title: 13. Container & Kubernetes Security
sections.description:
CONTAINER · K8S · POD SECURITY · IMAGE SCANNING

Staff+ expectation: Containers and Kubernetes are the standard deployment platform at FAANG. Each container is a security boundary. At Staff+ level, you design the cluster security architecture: image scanning, pod security policies, network policies, and runtime security.

Container imageContainer runtimeK8s APIPodNetworkSecrets
LayerThreatMitigationFAANG tool
Malicious base image, vulnsImage scanning, minimal base, signed imagesTrivy, Aqua, Sigstore
Container escape, kernel exploitSeccomp, AppArmor, SELinux, no privilegedgVisor, Kata
Unauthorized accessRBAC, audit logging, OIDC authK8s RBAC + OIDC
Privileged container, host mountPod Security Standards (restricted)OPA / Gatekeeper
Lateral movementNetwork policies, mTLS (sidecar)Istio, Cilium, Calico
Secret leakageVault sidecar, encrypted etcdExternal Secrets, Vault

Pod Security Standards (K8s 1.23+): Three profiles: (1) Privileged — unrestricted (for system components). (2) Baseline — prevents known privilege escalations. (3) Restricted — follows pod hardening best practices. At FAANG, the default is Restricted for user workloads. Open Policy Agent (Gatekeeper) enforces custom policies: "all containers must have CPU/memory limits", "no containers run as root", "no host network access."

Runtime Security (Falco, Aqua, Sysdig): Detects anomalous behavior at runtime: shell inside a container, unexpected network connections, file system writes to sensitive paths. At Google: every container has a seccomp profile that blocks unnecessary syscalls. At Meta: containers run in gVisor (a user-space kernel) for an additional security layer.

sections.title: 14. Developer Security: SAST, DAST & Security Champions
sections.description:
DEV SECURITY · SAST · DAST · CHAMPIONS · SHIFT LEFT

Staff+ expectation: The fastest way to improve security is to shift left — find and fix vulnerabilities before they reach production. At Staff+ level, you design the developer security toolchain, establish security champions, and create a culture where security is everyone’s responsibility.

SAST (Static Application Security Testing): Analyze source code for vulnerabilities without executing it. Tools: Semgrep, CodeQL (GitHub), Coverity, SonarQube. Run in CI, block merges with critical findings. At Google: Tricorder (internal SAST platform) runs 100+ analyzers on every change. At Meta: Infer (static analyzer) runs on every diff and catches null pointer, resource leak, and concurrency bugs.

DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): Test running application for vulnerabilities by sending malicious inputs. Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Acunetix. Run against staging environment before production deploy.

SASTDASTSCASecrets scanning
Tool typeExamplesFindsFPRIntegrates at
Semgrep, CodeQL, SonarQubeSQLI, XSS, hardcoded secrets, bad crypto10-20%Pull request (pre-merge)
OWASP ZAP, Burp SuiteRuntime injection, misconfig, exposed endpoints15-30%Staging (pre-deploy)
Dependabot, Renovate, TrivyVulnerable dependencies, outdated libraries5-10%PR + scheduled
GitLeaks, TruffleHogCredentials in code5%Pre-commit hook + PR

Security Champions Program: Not every team can have a dedicated security engineer. Security champions are engineers who (1) receive security training, (2) review their team’s security design, (3) triage security findings, (4) are the security point of contact for their team. At Google: every team has a security champion. At Amazon: security champions are a prerequisite for the "security review" gate.

sections.title: 15. Security Culture: Bug Bounties, Red Teams & Training
sections.description:
SECURITY CULTURE · BUG BOUNTIES · RED TEAM · TRAINING

Staff+ expectation: Security culture is what happens when security engineers aren’t looking. At Staff+ level, you build a culture where every engineer thinks about security, vulnerabilities are reported without fear, and the red team keeps everyone sharp.

Bug Bounty Programs: External researchers (and internal engineers) are paid for finding and reporting vulnerabilities. At Google: the Vulnerability Reward Program has paid $50M+ since 2010, with rewards up to $150K for critical findings. At Meta: bounties start at $500 and go to $100K for RCE. At Apple: the Security Bounty program pays up to $1M. Bug bounty ROI: every dollar spent on bounties saves $5-10 in incident response costs.

Red Team / Penetration Testing: Internal team simulates adversaries. Goals: (1) Test detection and response capabilities (the blue team). (2) Find vulnerabilities before external attackers do. (3) Validate security architecture. At Google: Project Zero (elite internal security team) has found critical vulnerabilities in every major OS. At Amazon: the red team runs continuous penetration testing against production systems.

Bug bountyRed teamPurple teamSecurity trainingSecurity reviews
ProgramScopeRewardFAANG example
External researchers$500-$150KGoogle VRP, Meta BBP
Internal adversarial simulationN/AAmazon, Google Project Zero
Red + blue collaborationN/AAll FAANGs
Internal engineersN/ASecurity Guild, CTF
Design review gateN/AMandatory before launch

Security Training: Every engineer at FAANG completes annual security training. Topics: (1) Phishing awareness (tested with simulated phishing campaigns), (2) Secure coding (OWASP Top 10, language-specific), (3) Data classification (how to handle PII, confidential data), (4) Incident response (who to contact, what to do). At Google: "Security 101" is mandatory for all new engineers. At Amazon: every new hire attends "Security Awareness" training within 30 days.

sections.title: 16. Staff+ Security Engineering Interview Strategy
sections.description:
INTERVIEW · STRATEGY · THREAT MODELING · INCIDENTS

Staff+ expectation: Security engineering interviews at FAANG test a mix of deep technical knowledge and architectural thinking. You’ll be asked to threat-model a system, design a secure architecture, and respond to security incident scenarios.

Common Staff+ Security Questions:

  • "Design a secure authentication system for a global service with 100M+ users." (AuthN/AuthZ, OAuth, MFA, session management, credential rotation)
  • "Threat-model our core payment system. Where are the vulnerabilities?" (STRIDE applied to a real system)
  • "A critical vulnerability (RCE) was found in a library used by 50 services. How do you respond?" (Incident response, containment, patching, communication)
  • "Design a secrets management system for 10K microservices." (Vault, dynamic secrets, rotation, encryption hierarchy)
  • "How would you secure a Kubernetes cluster handling sensitive data?" (Network policies, pod security, encryption, runtime security)

Company-Specific Emphasis:

  • Google: BeyondCorp, cryptographic protocol design, Project Zero mentality. Expect deep TLS, OAuth, and threat modeling questions.
  • Amazon: Leadership principles in security context. "Dive deep" on IAM policies, encryption, and incident response. "Are right a lot" on threat models.
  • Meta: At scale security: how do you secure billions of users with minimal friction? Expect questions on authentication, account security, and ML-based threat detection.
  • Netflix: Cloud security, automation, and security culture. How do you secure a cloud-native, multi-region infrastructure?

Security EngineeringThreat ModelingZero TrustCryptographyOAuth/OIDCK8s SecuritySupply ChainCloud SecurityIAMIncident ResponseApp SecuritySecrets
TopicResourceWhy for Staff+
